SYNOPSIS.
The story opens at Monte Carlo with
Col. Terence O'Rourke a military free
lance and something oi a gambler, in his
hotel. Leaning on the balcony he sees a
beautiful girl who suddenly enters the
elevator and passes from sight. At the
gaming table O'Rourke notices two men
watching him. One is the Hon, Bertle
Glynn, while his companion is Viscount
Des Trebes, a duelist. The viscount tells
him the French government has directed
him to O'Rourke as a man who would
undertake a secret mission. At his apart
ment, O'Rourke, who had agreed to un
dertake the mission, finds a mysterious
letter. The viscount arrives, hands a
sealed package to O'Rourke, who is not
to open it until on the ocean. A pair of
dainty slippers are seen protruding from
under a doorway curtain. The Irishman
finds the owner of the mysterious feet to
be his wife, Beatrix, from whom he had
run away a year previous. They are
.econciled, and opening the letter he finds
that a Rangoon law firm offers him
100,000 pounds for a jewel known as the
Pool of Flame and left to him by a dy
ing friend, but now in keeping of one
named Chambret in Algeria. O'Rourke
worsts the nobleman in a duel. The wife
bids O'Rourke farewell and he promises
to soon return with the reward. He dis
covers both Glynn and the viscount on
board the ship. As he finds Chambret
there it an attack by bandits and his
friend dies telling O'Rourke that he has
left the Pool of Flame with the governor
general, who at sight of a signet ring
given the colonel will deliver over the
ewel. Arriving at Algeria the Irishman
finds the governor general away. Des
Trebes makes a mysterious appointment,
and tells O'Rourke that he has gained
possession of the jewel by stealing it. In
a duel O'Rourke masters the viscount,
secures possession of the Pool of Flame
and starts by ship for Rangoon. He finds
the captain to be a smuggler who tries to
steal the jewel. It is finally secured by
the captain and O'Rourke escapes to
land. With the aid of one Danny and
his sweetheart, O'Rourke recovers the
Pool of Flame. On board ship once more,
bound for Rangoon, a mysterious lady
appears. O'Rourke comes upon a lascar
about to attack the lady, who is a Mrs.
Prynne, and kicks the man into the hold.
Mrs. Prynne claims she is en route for
Indiana on a mission for the king.
O'Rourke is attacked by the lascar, who
secures the Pool of Flame, the captain
is shot and the lascar jumps into the sea.
The ship arrives in port. Danny hands
O'Rourke the Pool of Flame which he
has stolen from Mrs. Prynne. It is the
real jewel, the one lost at sea being a
counterfeit. O'Rourke goes to Calcutta
and discovers Des Trebes disguised. He
now knows that Mrs. Prynne was an ac
complice. Finally he gets to the lawyer
who has offered the reward, delivers the
jewel and gets the money. Going to the
residence of the lawyer on invitation,
O'Rourke finds him murdered- and Des
Trebes is found dying.
CHAPTER XXXII.-(Cn.tinued.)
With each development the mystery
was assuming more fantastic propor
tions, becoming still more impene
trable and unsolvable. But he had no
leisure in which to ponder it now, if
Des Trebes were to be restored. And
O'Rourke worked over the man as
tenderly as though they had been life
long friends, with skillful fingers es
timnating the nature ano extent of his
wounds, with sound knowledge of
rough and ready surgery doing all
that could be done to bring him back
:to consciousness.
At last Des Trebes sighed feebly; a
spot of color, febrile, fickle, evanes
cent, dyed his cheeks; his breath rat
tled harshly in his gullet; his eyelids
twitched and opened wide. He glared
blankly at the face above.
"Des Trebes!" cried O'Rourke. "Des
rrebes!"
His voice quickened the intelligence
roe that moribund brain. A flash of
'recognition lighted the staring eyes.
The lips moved without sound.
"Des Trebes!"
"Ah, yes . . . the Irish
man . ."
The whisper was barely articulate.
O'Rourke put to his lips a cup of
brandy diluted with a little water.
"Drink," he pleaded, "and try to tell
me what's happened to ye. Who gave
ye these wounds? Try to speak."
"But . . . no . . . I shall
not tell."
"But-good God, man! ye've been
murdered!"
The white lips moved again; the
adventurer bent his ear low to them.
"We . . . have both . . . lost
. but you . .. your wife
"My wife!"
In a frenzy O'Rourke resumed his I
esfforts to strengthen the dying man 1
with spirits and water, but Des Tre- t
bes, with a final effort, obstinately e
shut his teeth, moving his head im- 1
perceptibly from side to side in token 3
of his stubborn refusal. I
So he died, implacable. In death t
the chiselled features remained set
in a smile sardonic and triumphant. r
Dying, he gave no comfort to his a
foe. .... e
Por a little time longer O'Roprke
knelt at Des Trebes' side, watching F
and wondering. Eventually he sighed
heavily, shook his head, shrugged his
shoulders and rose. And, rising, he e
perceived for the first time that he b
was no longer alone with the dead in ti
,that place. ti
Kneeling in silence by the vicomte's
Side he had till then been hidden from t
the inner doorway to the room by the f:
drapery of the center table. And evi
dently it was this circunistance which tl
Pad emboldened a man to slip in from k
the main hall and approach Sypher's ri
Peak at thI back of tho room. y
As O'Rourke appeared he was con
scious first of something moving in
the room-a movement caught vaguely
from the. corner of his eyes. Then he
heard a stifled cry of fright. He had
already his revolver in his hand, so in
stant had been the obedience of his
brain and body to the admonition of
instinct.
He swung about with the weapon
poised, crying: "Stop!" The other
man was apparently trying to escape
by the door to the hall, but was much
too far from it to escape the threat
ened bullet. A Jet of fire spurted from
his hand. O'Rourke heard a crash
and clatter of broken window-glass be
hind him. Without delay or conscious
aim he fired and saw, still indistinctly
through pungent wreaths of smoke,
the figure reel and collapse upon
itself.
The man had hardly fallen ere
O'Rourke stood over him, with a foot
firm upon one arm, while he bent and
wrenched a revolver from relaxing fin
gers. Then, stepping back, he took
stock of the murderous-minded in
truder, and saw at his feet, writhing,
coughing and spitting,, a Chinese
coolie-a type of the lowest class, his
face a set yellow mask, stolid, un
emotional, brutalized. Even then it
betrayed little feeling; only the slant
set black eyes burned with unquench
able hatred as they glared up at the
conqueror. . . . O'Rourke's bullet
had penetrated the man's chest; and
as he squirmed and groaned through
his sharpened teeth of a rat, a crim
son stain spread on the bosom of his
coarse white blouse.
Wholly confounded, O'Rourke shook
an amazed head. A third element had
been added to the mystery with no
effect other than to render it more
opaque and dense than before.
The telephone, its raucous voice
now long since stilled, came into his
mind, and he was minded to leave the
room and find it, to summon aid.
Before he could move, however, a
footfall on the Veranda startled him,
and his ears were ringing with a com
mand couched in terse, curt English:
"Hands up:"
CHAPTER XXXIII.
o -
f A man stood in one of the windows,
l his figure conspicuous against the
s night in cool white linen of a semi
military cut, his extended right hand
training a revolver on the Irishman's
s head.
f "Faith!" cried O'Rourke with genu
line relief, "you're more welcome than
t a snowfall in Hades. Good evening to
ye, and many of them."
"Hands up!"
"With all the pleasure in the world."
O'Rourke elevated his hands. "I've
s two revolvers on me person," he vol
I unteered amiably; "before ye go any
further ye'll be wanting to take 'em
away from me, I'm not doubting."
"From what I see, I quite believe I
shall," agreed the Englishman, with
I out relaxing his unprejudiced attitude.
"At all events, keep your hands where
they are, for the time being. .
What the deuce does this mean?"
"Tell me yourself and I'll make ye
a handsome present," returned the
O'Rourke composedly. "I've been ad
~ dling me wits over it for the last
thirty minutes, but neither rhyme nor
I reason can I read into it. But, see
now: would ye mind relieving me of
the arsenal I've been telling ye about,
that I may rest me arms without fear
of being punctured?"
The other laughed shortly and en
tered the room-a clean-limbed,
sturdy, well set-up boy of four or five
and-twenty, or thereabouts. He pos
sessed, aside from an emphatic and
capable manner, good looks enhanced
by a wide good-humored mouth.
"You might help me out a bit, you
know," said the boy briskly. "You've
been so free with your information
that I don't doubt you will place me
still further under obligation to you
by turning your back and depositing
your weapons on that table. Of course,
I needn't bore you by remarks upon
the folly of false moves."
"'Twould be quite superfluous," re
plied O'Rourke, obeying with a fair
and easy grace. "There now. What t
else may be your pleasure?"
"Move back three paces and stand t
still."
"Right-O, me lord."
O'Rourke executed the prescribed
evolution and, at rest, heard footsteps
behind him; a thought later he felt
the Englishman's hands rapidly going
through his pockets. Then, with a
"very good," the latter stepped be
tween the table and O'Rourke and c
faced him.
"You've apparently told the truth
thus far," he said. "Now what'dyou v
know about thin?" Ho waved a hand 1
round the roonr. "Be careful what 1I
you say. I u'niý: A well inform you a
I'm Couch, lieutenant sub-chief of
police for this district."
"Saint Patrick would be no more
welcome," declared O'Rourke. "I Was
on the point of trying to get ye by
telephone when ye saved me the trou
ble. How the divvle did ye happen
to drop in so opportunely?"
"I was coming up-stream in the po
lice launch, on the night tour of in
spection, and stopped at the landing
Just below this-the grounds here run
down to the river, you know-to tele
phone back to headquarters on busi
ness. The exchange,operator suggest
ed I look in here and see if everything
was all right-said he'd been unable
to get any response since nightfall. -
. Now?"
Carefully and concisely O'Rourke
wove the events of the day into a
straight narrative, starting with the
delivery to Sypher of the Pool of
Flame, touching briefly upon Des Tre
bes' part-so far as he understood it
-and concluding with the death of
the coolie. The sub-chief of police
eyed him throughout with gravely
concentrated interest, nodding his
understanding.
"I see," he said slowly. "You make
it clear enough. Moreover, you've
convinced me. I didn't really believe
from tie first you'd had any hand in
this ghastly mess, but I couldn't take
chances, of course. You're at liberty
to take up these pistols as soon as you
please; in fact, I advise you to do
so immediately. From what's taken
place already, you may have need of
'em within the next ten seconds. .
. Now for this coolie. If he's able to
speak, I'll get some information out of
him."
"'Tis too far gone he is, I'm fear
ing."
"We'll soon find out." The English
man bent over the man, who was now
very quiet, but, by the constant flick
er of his cunning eyes, still conscious.
A hasty examination told the investi
gator all he needed to know about tho
nature of the wound." "He'll not last
long," said Lieutenant Couch, and be
gan to converse with the local ver
nacular of Pidgin-English, about one
word in ten of which was intelligible
r
A n
FA \~ M
A Man Stood In One of the Windows.
to O'Rourke. As he continued to speak
the coolie's scowl darkened and he in
terrupted with a negative motion of
his head. The sub-chief repeated his
remarks with emphasis. For reply he
got a monosyllable that sounded, as
much as anything else, like an oath.
Couch looked up. "He says he wants
water, and I suspect he won't speak
until he gets it. Can you-?"
O'Rourke fetched the half-empty
carafe and Couch put it to the coolle's
lips, permitting him to drink as much
as he liked. But as soon as the bottle
was removed the fellow shut his
mouth likq a trap and refused a word
in answer to the lieutenant's demands
and uersuations.
"Stubborn brute," growled Couch.
"Most of these animals here belong to
some devlish tong or other, and they'd
rather die than say anything touching
on the business of the society or af
fecting the interests of a brother
member. But I think I know a way to
bring him to reason. Hand me that
knife, please."
Wondering, O'Rourke tendered him
the weapon that had brought death to
Sypher. The lieutenant wiped it cal
lously on a corner of the coolie's
blouse and held the keen shining
blade before his eyes, accompanying
the action with a few emphatic
phrases. A curious expression, com
pounded of sullen fury and abject pan
ic fright, showed in the Chinaman's
eyes, and his lips were as if by magic
unsealed. However reluctant, he be
gan to chatter and spoke at length,
delivering himself of a long state
ment which Couch punctured now and
again with pertinent, leading ques
tions.
At length, throwing aside the knife,
he jumped up, strong excitement burn
ing in his eyes. "I've got enough from
him," he said rapidly. "I'll explain
later. You'll help--of course; your
wife's involved as well as Miss Pyn
sent. But I don't think you need fear;
we'll be in time. Are you ready?
. Half a minute; I've got to use
that telephone."
He ran out into the hall, rang up
and shouted a number into the re
ceiver, and for a few moments spoke
rapidly in a Burmese dialect.
O'Rourke gathered that he was speak
ing with a native subordinate at the
police headquarters in Rangoon.
Couch swung back into the study.
"Got those revolvers, sir? Then come
along; we'll have to run for it. For
tunately our launch is handy; other
wise . . ."
He sprang across the veranda and
down to the lawn, O'Rourke pelting
after him.
CHAPTER XXXIV.
A night of velvet blackness, softly
opaque, lay upon land and water. The
police launch, shuddering with the vi
brations of a motor running at high
tension, sped down the silent reaches
of Rangoon River like a hunted ghost.
She ran Without lights, these having
been extinguished by Couch's direc
tions, regardless of harbor regulations
or danger. Happily the hour was late
enough to relieve them of much fear
of trouble with other craft; the upper
reaches of the river were practically
deserted.
In the bow Couch was handling the
wheel with the nonchalance of one
from whom the river had no secrets
by night or day. To O'Rourke it seemed
no light task to pilot so slight a craft
at such high speed through that
Stygian darkness; yet the sub-chief
was accomplishing the feat without a
discernable trace of fear or tremor
of uncertainty.
O'Rourke sat beside him. In the
stern a police orderly acted as me
chanic, attending to the motor. These
three, no more, made up the rescue
party.
Though devoured by impatience and
anxiety, O'Rourke forbore to question
Couch, hesitating to divert his atten
tion from his task and knowing that
as soon as he could the young lieu
tenant would speak. From the time
when the coolie had yielded, there
had been not a second's rest for eith
er; neither had had time to confer
save on questions of the most imme
diate moment; and control of these
Couch had voluntarily and naturally
assumed, deciding, acting and direct
ing in the same thought, apparently.
"Your wife, with Miss Pynsent,"
said Couch abruptly, without looking
round-"at least I presume it's Mrs,
O'Rourke, from what you say-have
been kidnaped by a gang of highbind
ers and are now aboard a junk in the
lower river, which will sail for God
knows-where at the turn of the tide.
That's the only thing that saves 'em.
We'll be on 'em before they're able to
force a way down the river."
O'Rourke groaned, holding his head
with both hands. "My wife . . .!"
he said brokenly.
"I know," Couch interrupted grim
ly; "I know how you feel. Miss Pyn
sent is there, too, you see."
"Oh," said O'Rourke, "I didn't un
derstand that. .. . I'm sorry." He
dropped a hand on the younger man's
shoulder and let it rest there briefly.
"Please God," he said reverently,
"there'll be many another polluted
yellow soul yammering at the gates
of hell this night!"
"Amen!" said Couch. . . . We
sha'n't be long now."
Silently O'Rourke removed his coat
and waist-coat, his collar and lawn
tie, and turned back his cuffs. "Even
ing clothes are hardly the thing to
fight in," he said; "but I'm thinking
'twon't make a deal of difference to
me. Got any cartridges for a Webley
mark IV?"
"Wheeler has. Give Colonel
O'Rourke a few, Wheeler," said Couch,
addressing the orderly.
The latter rummaged in a locker
and pressed into O'Rourke's hand half
a dozen cartridges, with which the
adventurer proceeded to replenish the
empty chamber in his revolver.
"I'd only discharged one," he ob
served, "but 'tis likely we'll need that,
even, with only the three of us
against a junk-load."
"Oh, I telephoned for reinforce
ments, of course," returned Couch.
"They ought to be there ahead of
us."
"What did the coolie tell ye, if ye've
time to talk?"
Couch laughed. "I daresay you're
wondering how I made him speak at
all."
"That's the true word for ye."
"I threatened to cut off his silly pig'
tail and send him naked and dishonor
ed to the ghostly halls of his ances
tors. It's wonderful how much those
callous brutes dote on that decora
tion. I told him further, that if he
lied, when I found it out I'd return
and shave him bald as an egg, even if
he were dead by that time. So I per
suaded the truth from him, the whole
story-from his side of it."
"I'm listening . . "
(TO BE CONTINUED.)
Mr. J. B. Duke's Ploughing.
In spite of the distractions of the
Tobacco company's reorganization, Mr.
James B. Duke bestows much atten,
tion upon the work of developing and
beautifying his three thousand acre es
tate, Duke's Park, near Somerville, N.
J. Not infrequently on his tours of
inspection he personally directs the
laborers. One day he took the plough
from the hands of a slow, awkward
foreigner, saying:
"Here, let me show you how to
plough a furrow. I've not forgotten
how I did that when I was a boy in
South Carolina."
Another day he took the place of
the boss of a gang of workLmen and
before he got through he dismissed
five for inefficiency.

NEWS OF MONTANA
Important Happenings of the
Week Briefly Told.
LOSE FIGHT FOR RICHES
Montana Woman Is Now Going Back
to Poverty.
Washington, D. C. - A broken.
hearted woman is going back to her
Pick husband and three babies-and
overty-in Butte, Mont., after losing
hard fight by six votes in the house.
he is Mrs. Katherine MacDonald.
Back in 1905 when the government
was building the Shoshone irrigation
dam she and her husband conducted
a grocery store at Butte. Following
the old custom they accepted as cash
for groceries the "time checks" which
contractors who worked on the dam
gave their laborers. But the con
tractors failed, and the MacDonalds
found they could not cash $11,000 of
the cheeks. Other people who held
$31,000 more in the checks ftbnd
themselves n similar position, Nhe
government didn't lose because the
contractors were heavily bonded, P.he
MaoDonalds, however, lost everyt.ing.
Then the husband was taken sek.
Mrs. MacDonald came on to Washo
ington, seeking passage of a bill grant
ing her and her husband the right to
secure a part of the contractor's bond
to make good their losses. The senat4
and house passed the measure. Presrt
dent Taft, however, held the leglasl
tion retroactive and he voted it. Mrs.
MacDonald, pleading, got sympathetle
senators to pass it over the veto. The
house failed to override the presa
dent's disapproval by just six votes.
RECORD CONTRACT LET.
Two Years Out of 85,000,000 Peet of
Timber Is Planned.
Kallspell.-The biggest logging conm
tract ever let in Northwestern Mon.
tana and probably in the state was
made between the Somers Lumber
company and H, W, Good. It calls foe
the delivery to the company of 35,000{
000 feet of logs, Two years are ak
lowed to complete the contract.
The timber is to be out twenty-five
miles northwest of Kalispell and will
be sent down the Stillwater river and
Flathead and across the upper bay on
the Lake of Somers.,
Three hundred men aitd fifty teams
will be employed in the logging opera
tions.
The contract is but one of the signs
of the return of the lumber industry to
its old form. The Somers company
will let additional contracts and othet
companies will have forces of men in
the woods.
COLONEL AT RALLY.
Dixon Trying to Arrange Visit With
Progressives During Convention.
Glendive. - The desire of Montana
Progressives to make their September
nominating convention in Helena,
when a full state ticket will be put in
the field, a great state rally day, bids
fair to be made a reality by the pres
ence of Roosevelt and Senator Joseph
M. Dixon of this state, the colonel's
campaign manager. A telegram from
Senator Dixon to Secretary James A.
Metcalf of the Progressive state cen
tral committee, says that he is trying
to arrange the colonel's itinerary with
this in view, though it may be neces
sary to change the convention date
from September 3 to a few days later,
DRY FARMING.
'Desert Land" Entryman Loses Clairm.
-Hundreds of Cases Involved,
Bozeman.-Word has been received
that the decision of the local land eo
flee in the case of Mrs. E. L, Houston
against B, W, Spaulding, a desert land
entryman, which, it is said, will affect
hundreds of homesteaders through the
west, has been confirmed by depart'
ment offloials at Washington,
Mrs. Houston's contention that the
land in question was not desert land,
as adjoining land of a similar char
acter was yielding profitable crops
without irrigation, was sustained. The
decision recognizes the modern meth
ods of dry farming.
LAND DECISION UPHELD.
Case Will Affect Hundreds of Home,
steaders In West.
Bozeman.-Word has been received
that the decision of the local land of.
fice in the case of Mrs. E, L, Houston
against B, W. Spaulding, a desert land
entryman, which, it is said, will affect
hundreds of homesteaders through the
West, has been confirmed by depart
ment officials in Washington. Mrs.
Houston's contention that the land in
question was not desert land, as ad.
Joining land of a similar character was
yielding profitable crops without irri.
gation, was sustained. The decision
recognizes the modern methods of dry
farming.
INDIANS ADOPT MEN.
.ouis W. Hill Becomes Gray Horse
and Charles Griffin Lame Bull.
Glacier Park,---Louls W, H1ill, chair
man of the board of directors of the
CrC'eat Northern railway, Harvey H.
iittner of Svlokune and Charles Griffin
of St. Paul, were adopted into the tribe
of Ulackfoot Indians at the celebration
of the night-of-black-tail at St. Marie's
lake, Hill became Gray Horse, litt'
ner was christened Mountain Chief,
and Griffin was given the name lame
Bull.

Letter from Winnemmuccs.
WontratrccA, J ttly 5, I8C9.
Editobm I.VDEi'EM>KNr :?The labor question
in attracting some attention among the labor
ing classes, and considerable notice is given
it in onr public prints. The importance and
necessity of coolie labor ure being studiously
impressed upon the public mind by nearly all
of tho newspaj>ers and pensioned scribblers
on the Pacific coast. The advantages to be
derived from this class of labor; the intel
ligence, industry, virtue, and docility of the
Chinese ruce, us pictured from day to dny by
these journals, are attracting the attention of
eastern manufacturers, planters, lumbermen,
and railroad companies. Already, the tel
egraph it forms us that hundreds of thou
sunas of Chinese slaves have been contracted
for in the Atlantic States. Our ocean steumers
plying between San Fnineisco and China, are
freighted with coolie laborers ami with the
rotten, venal carcasses of Cliinose prostitutes.
Our coolie orutors, coolie newspapers, and
coolie pimps become grandiloquent in their
jubilations, and gloat over the grand aud sub
lime scene. Whenever nil expression is
developed by nny of the leading uieu of our
]K>liticul parties that win l?o construed, by the
utmost stretch of the imagination, into favor
ing coolie labor, it is seized upon with the
utmost avidity and given special and con
spicuous notice in the coolie organs. If
the Kan Francisco pu]M-rs can be relied on in
this mutter, the several distinguished guests
from the Atlantic States found nothing so !
attractive, in all their journey, as the Chinese
institutions of the Pacific.
It would appear that the Committee of
Ways and Means, while at the Bay City,spent
the principal part of its time in the studying
of zoology and learning the peculiar tlavor
and quality of lizards, liorn-toad. tumble-bug
and grasshopper soups, with the chief Cel
estials, with a view of not only encouraging
Chinese immigration, but the introduction of
Chinese diet among the people, in order that,
by a well regulated system of tar ill's on these
luxuries, an immense revenue may lie derived
by the Government.
Imaginary outrages of the most revolting
character, perpetrated upon our moou-oyed

text:
SERIAL
STORY
The Pool
of Flame
—w—
i
By
LOUIS JOSEPH VANCE
Illustration* by Ellsworth Young
Copyright 1809, by Louis Joseph Vance
SYNOPSIS.
The si.iry opens at Monte Carlo with
Col. Terence O'Rourke, a military free
lance and something of a gambler. In his
hotel. Leaning on the balcony he sees a
beautiful girl who suddenly enters the
elevator and passes from sight. At the
gaming table O'Rourke notices two men
watching him. One is the Hon. Bertie
Glynn, while his companion is Viscount
Des Trebes, a duelist. The viscount tells
him the French government has directed
hi* to O'Rourke as a man who would
undertake a secret mission. At his apart
ment, O'Rourke, who had agreed to un
dertake the mission, finds a mysterious
letter. The viscount arrives, hands a
sealed package to O'Rourke, who Is not
to open It until on the ocean. A pair of
dainty slippers are seen protruding from
under a doorway curtain. The Irishman
finds the owner of the mysterious feet to
be his wife, Beatrix, from whom he had
run away a year previous. They are
reconciled, and opening the letter he finds
that a Rangoon law firm offers him
100,000 pounds for r jewel known as the
Pool or Flame and left to him by a dy
ing friend, but now in keeping of one
named Chambret In Algeria. O’Rourke
worsts the nobleman In a duel. The wife
bids O’Rourke farewell and he promises
to soon return with the reward. He dis
covers both Glynn and the viscount on
board the ship. As he finds Chambret
there is an attack by bandits and his
friend dies telling O'Rourke that he has
left the Pool of Flame with the governor
general, who fet sight of a signet ring
given the colonel will detlver over the
jewel. Arriving at Algeria the Irishman
finds the governor general away. Des
Trebes makes a mysterious appointment,
and tells O'Rourke that he has gained
possession of the Jewel by stealing It In
a duel O'Rourke masters the viscount,
secures possession of the Pool of Flame
and starts by ship for Rangoon. He finds
the captain to be a smuggler who tries to
steal the jewel. It is finally secured by
the captain and O'Rourke escapes to
land. With the aid of one Danny and
his sweetheart. O'Rourke recovers the
Pool of Flame. On board ship once more,
bound for Rangoon, a mysterious lady
appears. O'Rourke comes upon a lascar
about to attack the lady, who is a Mrs.
Prynne. and kicks the man Into the hold.
Mrs. Prynne claims she Is en route for
Indiana on a mission for the king.
O'Rourke Is attacked by the lascar. who
secures the Pool of Flame, the captain
is shot and the lascar Jumps Into the sea.
The ship arrives in port. Danny hands
O'Rourke the Pool of Flame which he
has stolen from Mrs. Prynne. It is the
real Jewel, the one lost at sea being a
counterfeit. O’Rourke goes to Calcutta
and discovers Des Trebes disguised. He
now knows that Mrs. Prynne was an ac
complice. Finally he gets to the lawyer
who has offered the reward, delivers the
Jewel and gets the money. Going to the
residence of the lawyer on Invitation,
O'Rourke finds him murdered and Des
Trebes is found dying.
CHAPTER XXXll.—(Continued.)
With each development the mystery
was assuming more fantastic propor
tions, becoming still more Impene
trable and unsolvable. But he had no
leisure In which to ponder it now, If
Des Trebes were to be And
O'Rourke worked over the mhn as
tenderly as though they had been life
long friends, with skillful fingers es
timating the nature ana extent of his
wounds, with sound knowledge of
rough and ready surgery doing all
that could be done to bring him back
to consciousness.
At last Des Trebes sighed feebly; a
spot of color, febrile, fickle, evanes
cent, dyed his cheeks; his breath rat
tled harshly In his gullet; his eyelids
twitched and opened wide. He glared
blankly at the face above.
“Des Trebes!” cried O'Rourke. "De3
Trebes! ”
His voice quickened the4ntelllgence
of that moribund brain. A flash of
recognition lighted the staring eyes.
The lips moved without sound. *
“Des Trebes!”
“Ah, yes . . . the Irish
man . .
The whisper was barely articulate.
O’Rourke put to his lips a cup of
brandy diluted with a little water.
“Drink.” he pleaded, “and try to tell
me what’s happened to ye. Who gave
ye these wounds? Try to speak.”
“But ... no ... i shall
not tell."
“But —good God, man! ye've been
murdered!”
The white Ups moved again; the
*dventurer bent his ear low to them.
“We . , . have both . . . lost
, . . but you . . . your wife .
is
“My wife!"
In a frenzy O’Rourke resumed his
efTorts to strengthen the dying man
with spirits and water, but Des Tre
bes, with a wih,. effort, obstinately
shut his teeth, moving his head lm
perceptibly from side to side In token
of his stubborn refusal.
So he died. Implacable. In death
the chiselled features remained set
•n u smile sardonic and triumphant.
BED MEN WERE INDUSTRIOUS
Their Method of Worxing Copper
Mines in the Lake Superior
Region.
The copper mines In the Lake Su
perior region were most important to
:he Indians When we remember that
V.ey did not understand the smelting
>i orea we can appreciate the value
>{ these mines. In them the native
nelal occurs both in small pieces and
S cat masses. The Indians had only
Dying, he gave no comfort to his
foe. . • .
For a little time longer O’Rourke
knelt at Des Trebes’ side, watching
and wondering. Eventually he sighed
heavily, shook his head, shrugged his
shoulders and rose. And, rising, he
perceived for the first time that he
was no longer alone with the dead in
that place.
Kneeling in silence by the vicomte’s
side he had till then been hidden from
the inner doorway to the room by the
drapery of the center table. And evi
dently It was this circumstance which
had emboldened a man to slip in from
the main hall and abroach Syphsr's
desk at the back of the room.
As O’Rourke appeared he was con
scious first of something moving In
the room —a movement caught vaguely
from the corner of his eyes. Then he
heard a stifled cry of fright. He had
already his revolver In his hand, so In
stant had been the obedience of his
brain and body to the admonition of
instinct
He swung about with the weapon
poised, crying: “Stop!” The other
man was apparently trying to escape
by the door to the hall, but was much
too far from It to escape the threat
ened bullet. A Jet of fire spurted from
his hand. O’Rourke heard a crash
and clatter of broken window glass be
hind him. Without delay or conscious
aim he fired and saw, still indistinctly
through pungent wreaths of smoke,
the figure reel and collapse upon
Itself.
The man had hardly fallen ere
O’Rourke stood over him, with a foot
firm upon one arm, while he bent and
wrenched a revolver from relaxing fin
gers. Then, stepping back, he took
stock of the murderous-minded In
truder, and-saw at his feet, writhing,
coughing and spitting, a Chinese
coolie —a type of the lowest class, his
face a set yellow mask, stolid, un
emotional, brutalized. Even then it
betrayed little feeling; only the slant
set black eyes burned with unquench
able hatred as they glared up at the
conqueror. . ’. . O’Rourke's bullet
had penetrated the man’s chest; and
as he squirmed and groaned through
his sharpened teeth of a rat, a crim
son stain spread on the bosom of his
coarse white blouse.
Wholly confounded, O'Rourke shook
an amazed head. A third element had
been added to the mystery with no
effect other than to render It more
opaque and dense than before.
The telephone, its raucous voice
now long since stilled, came into his
mind, and he was minded to leave the
room and find It, to summon aid.
Before he could move, however, a
footfall on the veranda startled him,
and his ears were ringing with a com
mand couched In terse, curt English:
“Hands up!”
CHAPTER XXXIII.
A man stood In one of the windows,
his figure conspicuous against the
night in cool white linen of a semi
military cut, his extended right hand
training a revolver on the Irishman’s
head.
“Faith!” cried O’Rourke with genu
ine relief, “you’re more welcome than
a snowfall in Hades. Good evening to
ye, and many of them.”
“Hands up!”
“With all the pleasure In the world."
O’Rourke elevated his hands. “I’ve
two revolvers on me person,” he vol
unteered amiably; “before ye go any
further ye’ll be wanting to take ’em
away from me. I’m not doubting."
“From what I see, 1 quite believe 1
shall,” agreed the Englishman, with
out relaxing his unprejudiced attitude
"At all events, keep your hands where
they are, for the time being. . . .
What the deuce does this mean?"
“Tell me yourself and I’ll make ye
a handsome present,” returned the
O’Rourke composedly. “I’ve been ad
dling me wits over it for the last
thirty minutes, but neither rhyme nor
reason can I read into It. But, see
now: would ye mind relieving me of
the arsenal I’ve been telling ye about,
that 1 may rest me arms without fear
of being punctured?”
The other laughed shortly and en
tered the room—a clean-limbed,
sturdy, well set-up boy of four or flve
and-twenty, or thereabouts. He pos
sessed, aside from an emphatic and
capable manner, good looks enhanced
by a wide good-humored mouth.
“You might help me out a bit, you
know,” said the boy briskly. “You’re
been so free with your Information
that 1 don't doubt you will place me
still further under obligation to you
by turning your back and depositing
your weapons on that table. Of course.
I needn’t bore you by remarks upon
the folly of false moves."
“ ’Twould be quite superfluous,” re
plied O’Rourke, obeying with a fair
and easy grace. “There now. What
else may be your pleasure?"
“Move back three paces and stand
still”
"Rlght-O, me lord.”
O’Rourke executed the prescribed
evolution and, at rest, heard footstep?
behind him; a thought later he fet
the Englishman's hands: rapidly going
through his pockets. Then, with a
"very good,* the latter stepped
tween the table and O’Rourke and
fa&sd him
to dig away the earth and smash the
rocks with stones some of which they
provided with a groove and attached
to a handle by means of a withe
around the groove
Archaelogists have found that they
also built fives upon the rocks ard
thus cracked them that they might j
more easily be smashed with ham
mers. One piece of copper propped
upon poles over fifteen feet below the
j surface of the ground was found by
explorers where it had beeu aban
. d-;ncd by iho Indian tjuafrymea
“You've apparently told the truth
thus far” he said. “Now what’dyou
know about this?” He waved a hand
round the room. "Be careful what
you say. I may as well Inform you
I’m Couch, lieutenant sub-chief of
police for this district.”
“Saint Patrick would be no more
welcome,” declared O’Rourke. “1 was
on the point of trying to get ye by
telephone when ye saved me the trou
ble. How the divvle did ye happen
to drop in so opportunely?”
“I was coming up-stream in the po
lice launch, on the night tour of in
spection, and stopped at the landing
Just below this—the grounds here run
down to the river, you know—to tele
phone back to headquarters on busi
ness. The exchange operator suggest
ed 1 look !n here and see if everything
was all right—said hs’d been unable
to get any response since nightfall. .
. Now ?”
Carefully and concisely O'Rourke
wove the events of the day into a
straight narrative, starting with the
delivery to Sypher of the Pool of
Flame, touching briefly upon Deß Tre
bes’ part—so far as he understood it
—and concluding with the death of
the coolie. The sub-chief of police
eyed him throughout with gravely
concentrated interest, nodding his
understanding.
“I see,” he said slowly. “You make
it clear enough. Moreover, you've
convinced me. I didn’t really believe
from the first you’d had any hand In
this ghastly mess, but I couldn’t take
chances, of course. You’re at liberty
to take up these pistols as soon as you
please; in fact, I advise you to do
so immediately. From whit’s taken
place already, you may have need of
em within the next ten seconds. . .
. Now for this coolie. If he’s able to
speak, I’ll get some information out of
him.”
“ ’Tis too far gone he Is, I’m fear
ing.”
“We’ll soon find out.” The English
man bent over the man, who was now
very julet, but, by the constant flick
er of his ••unning eyes, still conscious.
A hasty examination told the investi
gator all he needed to know about the
nature of the wound. “He’ll not last
long,” said Lieutenant Couch, and be
gan to converse with the local ver
nacular of Pidgin-English, about one
word In ten of which was intelligible
to O’Rourke. As he continued to speak
the coolie’s scowl darkened and he in
terrupted with a negative motion of
his head. The sub-chief repeated his
remarks with emphasis. For reply be
got a monosyllable that sounded, as
much as 'anything else, like an oath.
Couch looked up. "He says he wants
water, and I suspect he won’t speak
until he gets It. Cap. you—?”
O’Rourke fetched the half-empty
carafe and Couch put it to the coolie's
Ups, permitting him to drink as much
as he liked. But as soon as the bott'e
was removed the fellow shut nis
mouth like a trap and refused a word
In answer to the lieutenant’s demands
and persuasions.
"Stubborn brute," growled Couch.
“Most of these animals here belong to
9ome devllsh tong or other, and they'd
rather die than say anything touching
on the business of the society or af
fecting the Interests of a brother
member. But I think l know a way to
Weighing almost three tons, it was a
monument to the industry of the
North American Indians, who have too
often been considered lazy. am* who,
we must remember, had only the sim
plest tools with which to raise this
heavy mass. One of their shafts,
which contained a mass of copper
weighing several tons, was twenty-six
feet deep and the mass af copper had
been raised several feet, probably the
only means the Indians had of moving
it being wedges and sticks.
These were exceptionally deep
A.Man Stood in One of the Windows.
brlng him to reason. Hand me that
knife, please.”
Wondering, O’Rourke tendered him
the weapon that had brought death to
Sypher. The lieutenant wiped it cal
lously 1 on a corner of the cooiie'B
blouse and held the keen shining
blade before his eyes, accompanying
the action with a few emphatic
phrases. A curious expression, com
pounded of sullen fury and abject pan
ic fright, showed In the Chinaman’s
eyes, and his lips were as if by magic
unsealed. However reluctant, he be
gan to chatter and spoke at length,
delivering himself of a long state
ment which Couch punctured now and
again with pertinent, leading ques
tions.
At length, throwing aside the knife,
he jumped up, strong excitement burn
ing In his eyes. “I’ve got enough from
him,” he said rapidly. “I’ll explain
later. You’ll help—of course; your
wife’s Involved as well as Miss Pyn
sent But 1 don’t think you need fear;
we’ll be In time. Are you ready? .
. . Half a minute; I’ve got to use
that telephone."
He ran out into the hall, rang up
and sho.uted a number into the re
ceiver, and for a few moments spoke
rapidly In a Burmese dialect.
O’Rourke gathered that he was speak
ing with a native subordinate at the
police headquarters In Rangoon
Couch swung back Into the study.
“Got those revolvers, sir? Then come
along; we’ll have to run for it. For
tunately our launch is handy; other
wise . .
He sprang across the veranda and
down to the lawn. O’Rourke pelting
after him.
CHAPTER XXXIV.
A night of velvet blackness, softly
opaque, lay upon land and water. The
police launch, shuddering with the vi
brations of a motor running at high
tension, sped down the silent reaches
of Rangoon River like a hunted ghost.
She ran without lights, these having
been extinguished by Couch’s direc
tions, regardless of harbor regulations
or danger. Happily the hour was late
enough to relieve them of much fear
of trouble with other craft; the upper
reaches of the river were practically
deserted.
In the bow Couch was handling the
wheel with the nonchalance of one
from whom the river had no secrets
by night or day. To O’Rourke it seemed
no light task to pilot so slight a craft
at such high speed through that
Stygian darkness; yet the sub-chief
was accomplishing the feat without a
discernable trace of fear or tremor
of uncertainty.
O’Rourke sat beside him. In tbe
stern a police orderly acted as me
chanic, attending to the motor. These
three, no more, made up tbe rescue
party.
Though devoured by impatience and
anxiety, O’Rourke forbore to question
Couch, hesitating to divert his atten
tion from his task and knowing that
as soon as he could the young lieu
tenant would speak. From the time
when the coolie had yielded, there
had been not a second’s rest for eith
er; neither had had time to confer
save on questions of the most Imme
diate moment; and control of these
Couch had voluntarily and naturally
shafts, many others being smaller. In
one place an area of about 400 acres
had been worked over Judging from
the number of stone hammers cr
mauls found in a given bulk of the de
bris excavated from these ancient dig
gings thousands and thousands of ti e
hammers must have been used, and
may be found by clearing out the old
workings—Southern Workmen.
War Against Tea Drinking.
At Washington the heads of de
par tin cuts have declared war against
assumed, deciding, acting and direct
ing In the same thowght. apparently.
“Your wife, with Miss Pynsent,”
said Couch abruptly, without looking
round—“at least I presume it’s Mrs.
O'Rourke, from what you say—have
been kidnaped by a gang of highbind
ers and are now aboard a junk in the
lower river, which will sail for God
knows-where at the turn of the tide.
That’s the only thing that saves ’era.
We’ll be on 'em before they’re able to
force a way down the river.”
O’Rourke groaned, holding his head
with both hands. “My wife . . .1”
he said brokenly.
“1 know.” Couch Interrupted grim
ly; “I know how you feel. Miss Pyn
sent Is there, too, you see.”
“Oh,” said O’Rourke, “I didn’t un
derstand that . . . I'm sorry.” He
dropped a hand on the younger man’a
shoulder and let it rest there briefly.
“Please God,” be Bald reverently,
"there’ll be many another polluted
yellow soul yammering at the gates
of hell this night!”
“Amen!” said Couch. . . .We
sha’n’t be long now.”
Silently O’Rourke removed his coat
and waistcoat, his collar and lawn
tie, and turned back his cuffs. “Even
ing clothes are hardly the thing to
fight in,” he said; “but I’m thinking
’twon’t make a deal of difference to
me. Got any cartridges for a Webley
mark IV?”
“Wheeler has. Give Colonel
O’Rourke a few, Wheeler,” said Couch,
addressing the orderly.
The latter rummaged in a locker
and pressed Into O’Rourke’s hand half
a dozen cartridges, with which the
Adventurer proceeded to replenish the
empty chamber in bis revolver.
“I’d only discharged one,” he ob
served, "but ’tis likely we’ll need that,
even, with only the three of us
against a junk-icad.”
“Oh, I telephoned for reinforce
ments, of course,” returned Couch.
"They ought to be there ahead of
us.”
“What did the coolie tell ye. If ye've
time to talk?”
Couch laughed. “I daresay you’re
wondering how I made him speak at
all.”
“That’s the true word for ye.”
"I threatened to cut o(T his silly pig
tail and send him naked and dishonor
ed to the ghostly halls of his ances
tors. It’s wonderful how much those
callous brutes dote on that decora
tion. I told him further, that tr be
lied, when I found It out I’d return
and shave him bald as an egg, even If
he were dead by that time. So I per
suaded the truth from him, the whole
story—from his side of It.”
“I'm listening. . . .”
(TO BE CONTINUED.)
Growth of St. Petersburg.
That St. Petersburg is rapidly grow
ing in population is evidenced by tho
census taken in December, 1910, which
showed the population, including cer
tain suburban village’ formerly not
covered, to be 1,907,’ *. It Is pre
eminently an “office town” and also a
seaport for six or eight months of the
year.
The principal Industry Is the manu
facture of cotton textiles, although its
advantage as a port of entry for the
interior Is gaining recognition. A Una
of steamers has been established to
Libau, connecting there with a trans-
Atlantic tine to New York. This en
ables American shippers to send
goods direct to this port without the
delays of transshipment in foreign
ports.
i
Mr. J. B. Duke’s Ploughing,
In spite of the distractions or the
Tobacco company’s reorganization, Mr.
James B. Duke bestows much atten
tion upon the work of developing and
beautifying bis three thousand acre es
tate, Duke’s near Somerville. N,
J. Not infrequently on bis tours of
inspection he personally directs the
laborers One day he took the plough
from the bands of a slow, awkward
foreigner, saying
“ Here, let me show you how to
plough a rurrow. I've not forgotten
how I did that when I was a boy In
South Carolina."
Another day he took the place of !
the boss of a gang of workmen and
before he got through be dismissed \
five for Inefficiency.
Damning With False Praise.
William Dean Howells, the author,
was talking about a criticism that
praised nim almost fulsomely.
“I don’t quite like such indiscrim
inate praise.” he said “Such praise
does a man more harm than good.
It is like the enthusiastic policeman'a
remark about the genial new mayor.
“ ’Oh. he's a tine feller,' the police
man said. ‘Why, It don’t matter who
ye are. he’ll get drunk with ye.‘"
Had Faith In the Wireless.
Demonstration or the practical use !
of wireless telegraphy on water was j
first made just twelve years ago be
tween the Needles on the English
coast and the incoming steamship St.
Paul. The world doubted, but Mar
coni was positive. The St. Paul was
sixty-five miles off the coast when tho
first connection was established Ihe
wireless can now hash its message
over the ocean fully a.oou miles
the teadrinking habit, which, they
declare, prevents any business being
done during one hour of the after
noon. It is an infectious and demoral
izing habit Australian cricketers in
vented the “tea interval," a second

“What do you think of this scheme
of having the countries exchange chil
dien?” asked the Sewickley man.
’’l don't thluk anything about it"
raid the Wilkinsburg man. “What is

“An English family, for instance, ex

with a German family. References are
first exchanged and all that sort of

“1 see.”
“Thus both sets of children get a

It's quite a scheme."
“It’s an elegant scheme,” declared

Case ot Mistaken Identity.
President Taft was out for his aft
ernoon walk in Washington one day
when a flaxen-haired little girl ran out
In front of him, held up her finger,
and exclaimed, in a shrill voice:
“I know who you are!"
The president, thinking it not at all
nnusual that she should possess this
information, but willing to gratify her,
asked:
“Well, who am I?”
"Aw,” she said teasingly, "you're
Humpty Dumpty.”—Popular Maga
tine.

The International Congress on Ae

event of a birth occurring in an air
j craft the pilot is to enter the event
j in his log book and must notify the
fact to the authorities at the first
place at which he descends.”

“I then commenced to recommend U

“I’m sure I prescribe Postum as c.t

with the best results.

shall continue to use it and presen

“In convalescence from pneumor a,

INFORMATION ASKED.
The Pacific Railroad, running from Sacra
mento into the Sierras by way of Dutch
Flat is called the ‘‘Central Pacific.” The
road from the East, which is being pushed
with such wonderful rapidity towards our
Eastern borders, is called tbe‘‘Union Pacific.”
The Government subsidizes for this extends
all the way to our State line. There is un
derstood to be no good feeling between the
two companies and the latter will not con
nect with the former, but will seek to enter
California by some other route. Upon this
sul j eel the Goid Hill A'cws, some lime since,
contained a very significant article. We re
produce it here.
"It is reported that the Union Pacific
Railroad have got an injunction on the Cen
tral Pacific Company, restraining the latter
from laying rail ahead of continuity from
tide water. The Company can grade as
much, within 300 miles of continuous rail as
they may choose; hut are not allowed to lay
track. Such is the report. The Union Pa
cific is building road at the rale of two miles
a day; and themselves claim that they will
be at the Sierras with a locomotive, ten days
from New York, direct, during 1870, That
Company intend to make a ‘crossing’ in this
Stale and over the mountains into California,
on their own responsibility. They will adopt
the Placerville or Feather River route. If
the Placerville people get their road twenty
miles this-side of their city by 1869, their
central ‘transit’ will be accepted. Otherwise
the Feather River or Beckwith Pass route will
be adopted by the Union Pacific."
Apropos of the Central Pacific, a little
item has come to our knowledge, which
needs explanation. By the act of April, 1863,
among the conditions upon which tbe State
granted to that company a subsidy of $lO,-
000 per mile, was one that the material for
tbe construction of the State Capitol should
be transported over it free of charge, Tbe con
tract and plans of the building required it to
be built of granite, which was conveyed on
this railroad. Recently, however, Governor
Low and other Black Republicans, who have
the superiutoudency of tbe work, changed
the plans aud adopted brick for granite.
This releases tbe railroad from thousands of
tons of transportation. Now, the question
here occurs, was the change made from gran
ite to brick made by the Cominisioners, some
of whom are interested in the railroad,
prompted by a desire to benefit tbe State, or
was it to benefit tbe Railroad Company. We
hope some of our Black Republican contem
poraries will give us some light on tbe sub
ject.—Examiner.
U. S. Land Suit —The Commissioners of
the Land Office of this city were occupied
all day yesterday in taking testimony in tbe
casejof Dennis Johnson, claimant, vs. Patrick
Bannon, contestant, claiming the south west
quarter of section 8, township 9, range 9
east, located in El Dorado county, While
Oak township, on which is located a lime
kiln, and on which Bannon has been for tbe
last two years engaged in burning lime.
The testimony was all taken, and 20 days’
lime given to file brief and 10 days to an
swer.—Marysville Appeal.
_ jf®"A Radical member of ibe Wait Vir
ginia Senate is charged with stealing a bog,
and a member of Ibe House with “lifting" a

est men made laws fur the punishment of

Grasshoppers. —Grasshopper eggs have
made tbeir appearance in such quantities in
Cache Valley, Utah, as to threaten a desi

THE PLACER HERALD.
HOW CALIFORNIA 18 ROBIXD.
It baa usually bean supposed Ibat the
easiest mode to reach the understandings of
men is through the pocket nerve. Tbe most
obtuse can generally see when it is to their
pecuniary interest to do so. Party feeling
is a strong tie, but tbe love of the almighty
dollar is stronger. It frequently happens,
however, that prejudice, bigotry and fanati
cism blind tbe eyes of tbe most prudent and
calculating. An astonishing illustration of
this is tbe tenacity with which tbe toiling
millions of the land adhere to tbe Radical
party. It shows bow terribly their minds
have been poisoned against their Southern
brothers. We can understand how, during
the excitement of a ruthless civil war, when
passion runs wild, and ail tbs generous im
pulses and Christian bumaniliea are thrust
in tbe background, that tbe unthinking
masses might become oblivious (0 their true
interests. Bur after the smoke of battle has
cleared off and tbe land basks again in the
benificent smiles of peace, it is difficult
to undersand how they can be induced to
support a party, whose policy ia directly in
conflict with their material welfare.
Look for a moment at tbe working of the
present iniquitous tariff. It professes to be
an act to raise a revenue to eupport tbe gov
ernment, but in reality it ie a law to tax tbs
toiling millions of tbe land to swell tbe divi
dends of a few Eastern manufacturers. Now
suppose it was proposed to levy a direct tax,
say of six hundred millions of dollars annui
ally, to pay these manufacturing capitalists to
enable them to compete with European man*
ufacturers—is it supposed for a moment that
tbe people would stand ill There would be
sucb a combined and spontaneous uprising
all over tbe land, that tbe Rump Jacobins
would gladly flee to prieon wells to save
themselves. And yet if sucb a law were eot
tually passed, it would be nothing more or
less than wbat tbe present tariff does, under
tbe plausible yet deceitful name of protec
tion. The people are actually paying for
the privilege of buying inferior articles of
borne production, more than six hundred
millions of dollars annually as bounties to
tbe manufacturers. For every yard of cotton
and woolen goods' they consume, they pay
double wbat it would cost without the tariff.
In many instances they are required to pay
two and three prices upon articles of neces
sary consumption.
On California this infamous system of tax'
ing tbe many to support a few capitalists
falls peculiarly bard. Ilia nothing more or
less than tbe filching from us of more than
one third of our gold and other products
that we send abroad. Tbe present tariff
with tbe late increase on ibe number of ar*
lirles, averages about sixty per cent oo all
our imports. For convenience of calculation
we will call it fifty percent. When, there*
fore, we buy abroad SIO,OOO worth of goods,
we have to pay for them $15,000. This of
course deducts just one third from tbe value
of our gold, but it not only operates thus
on imported foreign articles, but also on
those of home make. Tbe price of these, of
course, supposing tbe quality to be tbe same,
must be quite as high as the foreign article
with tbe tariff, or otherwise tbe other could
not be sold at all, for men will buy where
they can buy tbe cheapest. We are sending
forward now large quantities of wheat and
think we ire doing well. All of ibis pays
for imported goods, foreign and domestic.
Thus suppose it nets us in New York $2 00
a bushel, in gold. We receive pay for it in
goods that are enhanced in value one third
because of protection alone, to say nothing
of the inflated rag-currency. In other words
we pay 101 l on our wheat, by getting a third
less than its value, just 66 2-3 cents per
bushel. Our gold is discounted at a similar
rate. And this drain upon ua—this system
of unrighteous robbery—has been going on
for years, and yet the greedy protectionists
still cry for more and the people sustain
them.
Mr. Ferris, in his carefully prepared and
able work on the finances of tbe United
Slates, which we noticed sometime since,
gives some striking illustrations of the work
ings of this ruinous system. “Tbe people of
this Stale,” be says, “suffer under the burden
of their taxes, but a large amount of tham
are out of sight. They pay them in ways
which they do not comprehend. At present
tbe mines of California are yielding about
$48,000,000 a year. Tbe custom booia
takes one third from the value of gold for
foreign use. In a subsequent paper I pro
pose to demonstrate that quite ea large an
amount is taken from its value by Federal
legislation, for domestic usee as money.
Hence we are justified in fully
one third the value of all tbe gold yield of
the mines for and on account of such legis
lation. Such being tbe case let us see wbat
California pays aud lose* for the good of the
Union*..
Annual yield of gold $48,000,000; low
on this gold, ns above demon
strated, one third $10,000,000
Customs collected in Son Francisco
for the fiscal year just closed.... 7,134,664
Internal revenue, $6,732,387 curren
cy, equal to. 6,154,925
Grand total $28,289,689
“Sucb is the annual tribute California pays
for the support of the Union at large, and it
ia equal to eighty dollere for every one ia
the State. Tbie, however, ia only tbe Na<
tional claim. Tbe State, county and city
expanses are some four par cent, further.
No wonder then with such draft*, occult
and patent, upon the resources of the people,
they cry out hard times. California be*
paid in limes past to the General Govern,
ment, in losses on her gold through the
aoliou of tbe tariff, and interest on these
losses $419,333,433 — enough to build four
railroads from this State to the Missouri
AUBURN, PLACER COUNTY, CALIFORNIA, APRIL 20, 18G7.
River. She is paying to tbe Federal Gov
ernment and losing on her gold through tbe
action of the tariff, more than $28,000,000
per year, enough to build the railroad from
California to the Missouri River with a dou
bla track tbe whole distance, and equip it
with rolling stock, locomotives and appoint*
meats, every five years ; —yes, reader, every
five years, California is paying enough in
gold, adding to it tbe losses which tbe tariff
makes on her coin, to do all this immense
work.”
And yet tbe people, still blinded to tbeir
interests, reject the Democratic theory of
free trade, and adhere to a party which not
only fleeces them thus, but whose leaders are
still bent 00 increasing tbit infamous rob
ber’s toll or tariff. The bill only failed in
the late Rump Congress because the bounty
•berks could not agree upon nn equalization
of tbe plunder. Sometimes rouges fall out,
and to this alone are we endebted for being
spared another raid upon our products.
Should not tbe people be sick of this abom
inable end costly idol, mongrelisml—Exam
iner.
Novel Method of Managing a Restive
Horse. —Recently n groom, mounted on a
high mettled hunter, entered tbe High street
of Coldstream, England, end when opposite
Sir John Majorbank'e monument, the borse
began to plunge end rear to n fearful extent,
swerving to tbe right and then to tbe left,
but go forward be would not, nor could all
tbe exertions of tbe groom overcome bis ob
stinacy. Tbe street was filled with people
expecting to see the animal destroy himself
on tbe spikes of tbe iron railing around the
monument, when Mr. McDougal, saddler,
walked up to the groom and said: “I think
my man, you are not taking tbe proper
method to make tbe borse go; allow me, it
you please to show you e trick worth know,
ing." “Well,” saye tbe groom, "if you can
make him go, it’s more than I can,” when
Mr. McDougal took a small piece of whip
cord, which be lied in a firm knot in tbe ani
mals ear, which be bent gently down, fasten
ing the end of tbe string to tbe check buckle
of the bridle, which done, be patted tbe
horse's neck ouen or twice, and said, "Now
let me see you go quickly home like a good
borse,” and, astonishing to relate, it moved
off as gently as if nothing bad happened.
Mr. McDougal says he baa aeen, in Loudon,
horses which no manner of force could make
go, while this mild way was always success
ful.
Pacific Railroad.—A New York tele*
gram of March 22d, aays that a 300-mile sec*
lion of this road is let, end to be completed
in 100 days from May Ist. There will then
be e continuous line of railway from New
York to the mining region of Colorado Ter
ritory; leaving only about 900 miles to com
plete tbe chain linking ocean to ocean.
The largest plough factory in tbe world is
at Louisville (Ky.). During tbe working
yean aince 1860, including the present sea
son, the factory has turned out over two
hundred thousand plows, and has, at tbe
present time, a capacity for one hundred
thousand a year.
A Challenge. —Captain W. U. William
son of Mobile has purchased the race horse
“Harry of the West," and challenges any
borse in tbe United states to meet him in
the Spring races for any sum between $lO,-
000 and $25,000.
The Vallejo Railroad, —The Marysville
Appeal of the 28tb is informed that grading
on tbe Vallejo Railroad baa beau renewed
with greet vigor since the pleasant weather
commenced. Three hundred bends and one
hundred teams era now employed in the
work, end tbie number will be increased as
tbe season advances.
Prentice vs, FoRSEY.—Uere is Prentice’s
last bit at Forney, and it is a hard one:
A friend of Forney says be “grew like a
rough oak among storms and whirl winds.”
Never mind, Forney, you’ll probably be a
hot bouse plant in tbe next world.
The Mineral Land Law. —U. S. Survey
or-General Upson has appointed R. M. Wil
son, of Tuolumne county, U. 8. Deputy Sur
veyor for Miuerel District No. 8, comprising
tbe counties of Tuolumne, Mariposa, Fresno,
Merced end Stanislaus. Office at Sonora,
Tuolumne county.
Arreogeme nts have been made by which
Odd Fellows in Suisun, Vallejo, Nape, Sono
ma, Sants Rosa and Healdsburg will unite in
celebrating tbe 26tb of April at Vallejo,
There will be a procession, orations by W.
W. Pendergast of Napa, and J. A. Brewster
of Sonoma, and a poem, a dinner, and a ball
in tba evening.
Natural Gas.—We understand that ell
tbe oil boring works in tbe Coast Range
foothills, Colusa county, have ceased but
one. Tbe well of the company io operation
baa been aunk to tba depth of about 500
feet, and which yield* a steady supply of a
!*ood illuminating gas, and pipes are being
aid for tbe purpose of lighting a mill
with it.
Tan New Gab. —Tba new gas made from
wood and animal refuse bee been tried in
the Mission Woolen Mills, San Francisco
and burns well. Tbe owners of the patent
offer to light cities at five cents e light per
night, if more then one hundred lights be
used, and if leu than one hundred, at ten
cents per night. Sacramento can afford to
take one hundred and fifty at seven and a
half dollars per night, or two thousand sev
en hundred dollere per annum, if tbe lights
are need night, Summer and Winter, moon
or no moon.
Minors’ Protection League.
The regular monthly meeting of this or
ganisation was held at the Secretary’s on
Saturday Inst. The President having called
the meeting to order, the Secretary rend the
following repoit:
Forest Uii.l, March 30, ISC7.
“2b the Members of the Miners' Protection
League — Gentlemen: —Agreeably to Section
7 ot tbe Constitution, your Directory have
the honor to other their monthly report for
March.
"During the present month tbe working
men of Michigan Bluff have organized a
League, under the name of ‘Anti-Coolie
League,’ having the same objects in view as
the ‘Miners’ Protection League,’ and from
tbe Secretary, A. Longley, a letter bas been
received inviting the approval and co-opera
tion of this League.
“Your Secretary has placed himself in
correspondence with the Secretary of the
‘Central Pacific Anti-Coolie Association,’
of San Francisco, and obtained from them
a copy of their Constitution and By-Laws,
as adopted at a mass meeting, held at the
American Theatre, in that city, on the 61 h
instant. Your Directory find that there is
no conflict between tbe Constitutions o( Ihe
two associations; their views are similar,
tbeir interests identical; but tbe provisions
of tbe San Francisco organization are more
stringent than tbe laws of our own. Tbeir
Btb and 9lb resolutions require your consid
eration. They read as follow;
Resolved, That all laborers, mechanics,
merchants, and other citizens throughout
tbe United Slates, who sympathize with us
in our efforts to protect the labor of free
while men against the degrading and blight)
ing influence of Mongolian competition, are
earnestly invited to organize themselves into
Clubs or other Associations, for tbe purpose
of aiding by unity of action in driving for
ward tbe good work of saving our Continent
to the white race and the cause of civili
zation,
Resolved, That wc hereby extend an earn
est and cordial invitation to all those
throughout tbe State of California who
sympathize with us in our opposition to the
use of Coolie labor in this Stale to organize
themselves at once into associations, upon
a basis similar to tbe following, and to
send d> legates, to assemble in Con
vention, at the city of San Francisco,
on the Blb day of May, 1867, for tlie pur
pose of agreeing on some general plan of
action, io harmony with and for the better
carrying out of tbe foregoing declaration of
principles.
“Your Directory sincerely hope that tins
invitation will meet with a hearty response
throughout California, and that the Conven
tion of tbe 81b May next will place the en
tire State in position to combat with united
force the great evils of Coolie labor.
“Your Directory recommend that this
League should elect (wo delegates to repre
sent their interests in this Convention, who
shall be duly instructed, as to the wishes of
tbe League, and armed with discretionary
power to act fur its best interests, and are of
the opinion that the present is none too soon
to nominate, and elect such delegates aud
make preparations fordefraying the expenses
thereby occasioned,
"Your Directory regret to state that quite
a number who heartily sympathize with this
movement, are deterred by the throats of
employers of labor not to give work to mem
bers of tbe League, aud one instance bas
come under notice, of nn employer asking
as a requisite to the engagement of a labor
er: ‘Uow do you stand on the Chinese
question?’ These circumstances are of deep
er import than might at first appear to the
superficial observer. They are not only in
tended to defeat and frustrate the objects of
tbe League, but they clearly indicate the
entering of the wedge, for a far more seri
ous evil, viz: that of controlling the votes of
the laboring classes —virtually disfranchising
them. Is tbe boasted political liberty guar
anteed by the Constitution of the United
Stales to every citizen to be thus destroyed?
Will the working men in California tamely
submit to Lave their privileges, their heredi
tary and acquired rights taken away by the
bold grasping band of capital? It is but
another of tbe demoralizing effects of Coolie
labor—a deliberate attempt to grind down
tbe laboring classes, so as to reduce them to
tbe lowest possible position, and use their
own votes to still further oppress them.
“A mining claim with mill, etc., at Yan
kee Jim’s, bas changed bands since the last
report of your Directory, and tba now firm
partly composed of citizens of Forest Hill,
have commenced work with a gang of Chi
nese laborers, which indicates that the em
ployment of Chinese will be as generally
adopted in this neighborhood as circumstao
oes will permit; and suggests the importance
of this League being strengthened and sup
ported by every working man, who has any
hope for maintaining tbe position and free
dom be has hitherto enjoyed.
“It does not appear desirable for this
League to commit itself to any definite
course of action till after the session of the
Convention ou the Blh of May, when the
future policy of the working population of
California shall be agreed upon, and the
entire Slate organized so as to act in unison.
But let the working men consider that with
them rests the power to secure fur them
selves their homes, and means of providing
for tbeir families, or passing over their
homes and occupations to the Mongolian
race. Let them not be deceived by the
specious arguments and persistent declara
tions of those who, in face of every evidence
to tbe contrary denounce this movement, as
‘Copperhead,’ and use every influence to
deter tbe working men who have been and
are ‘Republicans’ from joining in laudable,
and reasonable efforts to protect themselves
from the aggression of those capitalists, who
would destroy the future greatness and
prosperity of California, and stifle tbe voice
of honest white labor by making this move
ment a party cry, and assuming to the Rei
publican party (lie unenviable position of
being the champions of Coolie labor, and
the introducers of Chinese slavery with all
its hosts of concomitant evils, to the utter
disregard of ourselves, our own while rising
generations, and the many intelligent while
men and women, both in America and Eu
rope, who would, but fur such Chinese slav
ery, seek our shores to the improvement of
tbeir own private resources and also to the
improvement of our population and tbe en
hancement of the wealth of our Stale.
“Your Directory, therefore, call upon ev
ery man irrespective of political sector parly
to place himself in the ranks of the League,
and aid by bis presence and support, in
averting the inoroadimenls of Chinese
against white labor, believing ibat this
question not only applies to the laboring
man but to men of every trade and profes
sion who would really cousult their own true
interests aud the future prosperity of Cali
fornia.
“Messrs. Win. Norlwood and J. B. Rus
sell having failed to attend the meetings
of the Directory, their seats were declared
vacant under the oth Section of the By-
Laws, and Messrs. I’billip Brown and Frank
Suling have been duly elected iu their stead.
“We have the honor to be your most
obedient servants,
J. T. Humphreys. President.
F. R J. Dixon, Secretary.”

Barnacles. — Barnacles are small shills
which gather in bunches ou the bottoms of
vessels,perforate tbo wood, and, if not scrape-1
off, finally destroy the vessel.— A Radical
paper says its party is a craft in bad condi
tion, covered with bran ados, and needs scra
ping off. The Sacramento Union indicates
ibe same thing, and is disposed (0 scrape off
the chief of the Short Hairs and his followers.
We think the Radical craft filled, aud thick
ly, too, with greedy branacles. It contains
more unprincipled men, more corrupt men,
more mischief makers, more audacious trai
tors to the country, more apologists for crime,
more violators of law, more sycophants, more
slaves of parly, more worshipers of wealth,
and more otlicehoiding rascals than any par
ty that ever existed in this or any oilier
country. —“None but itself can be its paral
lel."—History furnishes nothing approach
ing it iu Ibis respect. The man who will
lake passage on such a craft is either reck
less, dishonest or insane. But the old ship
Democracy is a well appointed ironclad, up
on which branacles can have no effect. She
was launched nearly seventy years ago, and
bas been in the government service most of the
lime ever since. She is freighted with the
hope, prosperity and happiness of republic
can government in this country, and fuilb
fully have they been guarded beneath the
starry flag she floats. She has weathered
tempestuous seas without suffering serious
damage.— When her crew divided in 1860,
and thus rendered her powerless, she was
put out of the government service, and the
piratical, and now barnacled, Abolition craft
was run in. She has made sad havoc of
tbe affairs of tbe country. After a most
disastrous voyage of six years, she has
been declared unseaworlby by her ablest
and most experienced captains, Johnson
aud Seward, who have abandoned her to the
lender mercies ot the barnacles. Tbe hull
of the old Democracy is just us solid, her
bottom is as sound and her rigging as pro
perly arranged as when captain Jefferson
first set foot upon her clean and finished
decks. She is about to be returned to the
government service, to tbe great joy of the
faithful who have never forsaken her, and of
tbe masses who have been plundered by the
piratical Abolition craft. Secure your pas
sage while the books are open. The old
craft is getting crowded with men who have
bedb led astray by false professions,—Moun
tain Democrat.

cuit Court, Judge Dooley presiding, is of
great force and precedent, as It is now ju
dicially settled in this State that a tax bill
title is tbe best. The secondary effects of

announces in the Spectator that be remitted
on the 9th inst., to Tbos. O. Summers, D D.,
Nashville, Tennessee, three thousand twenty
seven dollars and fifty cents ($3,027 60) in
gold coin for the widows aud orphans of tho
South, to be (lisliibuled by tbe Relief Com
mittees in (ho must destitute localities. As

Vegetable Flannel. —This article ia
manufactured in Germany from the pinut
silvestris. There are a large number of
persona now engaged in tbe Black Forest
in the various processes of separating tba
oil and the fiber, called waldwolle, or foreet
wool, from the pine leaves, and in spinning,
and weaving and knitting the yarn. This
vegetable flannel is believed to have the im
portant properly of re-establishing the func
tions of the skin when they have been im
terfered with by accidental causes.
Kansas is to take a vote on the female
right of suffrage amendment to the Consti
tution, at the next general election; both
Houses of (be State Lsgislature having pas
sed a bill to that effect.
Price of the Land. —Tbe territory which
it is proposed to purchase from Russia, con
tains 481,270 square miles, or 308,016,640
acres of land. The price to be paid is $7,-
000,000, or say a fraction over two cents
per acre. The territory purchased from
Mexico by the United States, after the war,
contains 202,800,000 acres of land, and cost
$15,000,000, or say a fraction over nineteen
cents per acres.
Frozen Men. —The Montnna Democrat,
says, during the recent cold snap many par
ties have come into this city, rather -badly
frozen, but most of them are improving.
We regret to learn that there has been much
suffering over in tbe direction of Salmon
River, but as tbe weather is getting milder it
ia hoped the winter is over.
Dr. Downes. —Tbe Lakeport Courier
states (bat Dr. Downes, lately wounded
while aiding an officer in arresting a man,
bas so far recovered from bis injuries as to
be to walk about without assistance.
The Stocklonians have determined to
construct a graveled highway from their
city to French Camp, a distance of some five
miles.

THE MARBLE BOOSTER
