text:
ALASKAN ABPRIGINEES
Harass the Migratory March
the "Boston Men."
of
Governor Swineford Puts Some Politi
cal Capital Out at Interest by
Arresting the Chief of
the Chilkats.
The Citizens of Juneau and Douglas Is
land Bise in their Might and Declare
that the Chinese Must Go.
Coolie Laborers Fxpelled from the
Treadwell .Mine Without Violence.
I SPECIAL HERALD CORRESPONDENCE. 1
Seattle, \V. T.. August 15,1886.
To the Editor of the Herald.
I Sir:— As your readers may wish for a
reliable statement of facts connected not
only with the recent arrest of the Chilkat
chiefs by Governor Swineford, but also of
the expulsion of Chinese from Juneau City
and Douglas Island, I take the liberty of
presenting them with the following:
About the 15th ultimo, Archbishop
Sehers, with a party ot live, reached
Chilkoot, rn route to the headwaters
of the Yukon river. The Chilkat
Indians had already become vexatious
about the rush of "Boston men" to the in
terior of Alaska, as they l»elieve (correctly,
too,) it will interfere with their fur trade.
As they had not heard of the coming of
Bishop Seghers and knew nothing about
the nature of his mission, they necessarily
regarded his party in the same manner as
those already north of the Chilkoot divide.
The Bishop has, as a matter of course, been
subjected to much annoyance. The Chil
kats would agree to pack his supplies over
the pass for so much and then would break
the bargain time and again, raising the price
each time. However, the Bishop's party
were dually taken over the Chilkoot pass.
As a matter of course, the Bishop sent let
ters to friends in Juneau, complaining of
the attitude of the Indians, especially of
the'cbief, Chieklaunt.
On the arrival of the steamship Idaho at
Juneau with Governor Swineford aboard,
<71 route to Sitka, the Bishop's encounter
with the Chilkats was necessarily brought
to the Governor's attention. He being
A PURE AND SIMPLE POLITICIAN,
seized the opportunity for the noise which
it will create in his behalf, rather than for
the 'interest of the inhabitants ot the Ter
ritory, and at once determined to arrest
the Chilkat chief, and accordingly sum
moned a posse, under a special U. S. deputy
marshal, fully armed, to accompany him to
Chilkat (where the Idaho had to call) for
that purpose. . On arriving there, it was
thought best, however, to invite the chief
tain aboard the steamer, which was done.
Not suspecting trouble from the ''Boston
men,'' he boarded the Idaho, when he was
seized and placed in irons. "During the
operation," it is said, "Chieklaunt stood
erect, with a smile of scorn playing around
his mouth, and only said, "Boston men him
d— cowards.'" He was then taken to
Sitka, where information will be tiled
charging him with an offense in accord
ance with the foregoing facts.
I must here say that several well in
formed pioneers at Jnneau called upon the
Governor before his departure for Chilkat,
and suggested to him the propriety of
adopting the plan, which Captain Glass in
troduced, and which brought about good
results, namely, appointing arbitrary chief
tains to positions in our service. These
pioneers were and are of the opinion that
the Governor should have made Chieklaunt
a police captain, with the uniform and pay
of such, which would beyond doubt make
him a good and useful Indian. Such action,
however, would have made no noise for
Swineford. As the case now looks, the
cruel arrest of Chieklaunt may yet end in
an Indian outbreak, and thls is just what
the politician could most wish for.
Iu a former communication I had occa
sion to allude to the employment of
CHINESE LAHOR
by the Alaska Mill & Mining Co., Douglas
Island, Alaska. Owing to many circum
stances peculiar to the district, their pres
ence proved exceedingly annoying to the
inhabitants of Juneau City and Donglas
Island. They say the places of the coolies
could and can be filled by the natives at
the same rate of wages, and the latter can
do far more work and perform it equally
as well. These Indians are industrious,
and, belonging to the Territory, they have
an interest in its development ; therefore
they should be employed in preference to
aliens who are foreign to us morally, po
litically and otherwise. The situation was
quietly talked over by the citizens of Doug
las Island and Juneau City. A public
meeting was finally convened at Juneau on
the 4th inst., whereat the expulsion of all
coolie labor from Southeastern Alaska was
carefully considered. The adoption of the
following preamble and resolution, and the
appointment of a committee of fifty miners
and residents of Juneau City and Douglas
Island gave form and force to the move
ment.
The preamble and resolution adopted at
the anti-Chinese meeting, Juneau City,
August 4, 1886, were as follows :
Whereas, the employment of coolie
lalior as miners and otherwise by the
Alaska Mill A Mining Company. Douglas
Island, Alaska, is decidedly detrimental to
the interest of the white miners and resi
dents of Douglas Island and Juneau City,
and that their presence among us is, and
has been, the cause of keeping American
miners out of employment, therefore l*e it
Mcaolred, That a committee of titty min
ers hd< 1 residents ot Juneau City and Doug
las Island lie appointed by this meeting to
wait upon the general superintendent ol
the Alaska Mill A Miuing Company and
inform him of the action of this meeting,
its aggressive intention, and that the com
mittee use all legitimate means possible to
enforce the dismissal of all coolie labor
employed by said company and to hasten
their departure from our camps.
The foregoing preamble and resolution
were adopted and signed by all the mer
chants of Jnneau, minus two, and by the
remainder of the male inhabitants, except
seven or eight saloon keepers and gamblers.

Having arranged for their trip to the
island, the committee at 10 a. m. on the
5th got into several canoes and shoved off
for the island, distant two and a half miles.
After landing, they were reluctantly re
ceived by Mr. Treadwell. The committee
having selected Messrs. Wheelock and Tim
mens to speak in behalf of the citizens,
these gentlemen not only informed the
superintendent of the desire of the inhab
itants of Junean City and Douglas Island,
bat also gave him many reasons for the
dismissal of the Celestials from the com
pany's employ. He finally replied: "These
Chinamen are here under a contract, which
will expire in a few weeks. If they should
then leave the company's employ, I shall
be at liberty to fill their places with whom
I please. Under no circumstances, how
ever, will I ever consent to their expulsion
before the expiration of our contract with
them. In the meantime I shall hope to be
able to protect them from all attacks."
The representatives of the people remon
strated with him, urging the expediency of
their immediate dismissal. Treadwell,
however, declining to entertain the propo
sition, the committee returned to Juneau,
when they made a report to a special meet
ing. After the reception of the report and
discussion thereon, the meeting went into
executive session, which means that the
door was then closed against all employers
of coolie labor. After the adjournment of
this meeting, there was but one public
statement made that night: "It is settled;
the Chinese must go."
About 9 a. m. of the 6th, one hundred
miners and residents assembled on the
beach, and while they were securing canoes,
many said to me: "We are instructed to
! use every means, except the destruction of
i life and property, for the immediate ex
pulsion of Treadwell s slaves, and if we
are not mistaken, you will see them on the
wharf this evening, waiting for the steam
; ship Idaho. About 10 a. m. the citizens
I committee shoved off, and as far as I could
I see, there was not a siDgle gun or pistol
taken to the Island. After their arrival
i there, Treadwell, though agitated, received
I them kindly, saying, "Well, gentlemen,
what are you after ?"
Mr. Wheelock replied, "We are after your
slaves, and propose to return with them."
Treadwell then said: "Gentlemen, what
ever you do towards their expulsion
YOU WILL DO AT YOUR PERIL.
Timmens replied- "We are living neither
in chains nor in darkness, and therefore
yonr Chinese must go."
The committee then moved on to the
mine, where a number of Chinese were
employed. As soon as the committee ex
plained their mission, the Chinese were not
only ready but anxious to leave. About
this time, Treadwell specially requested
the committee to allow the coolies to work
until 5 p. m., when he would see that they
should be ready for embarkation. Of course,
the request was granted.
To the great delight of all parties, ex
cept Treadwell A Co., the committee landed
seventy-six coolie laborers on the wharf at
Juneau City that 6:45 p. m.. leaving only
three coolie bosses on the Island to [settle
their business with the Alaskan Mill A
Mining Company, who agreed to be ready
to leave the Territory on board of the next
steamer, two weeks hence. So far, these
citizens had accomplished the removal of
the Chinese from Donglas Island without
the commission of a single act of violence,
unless their expulsion, no matter how ob
tained, may be regarded as such.
After the Celestials were located nicely
on the wharf, the agents of the steamship
company informed the committee that the
steamship Idaho had been seized lor carry
ing more passengers than she is allowed by
law; that she is closely watched: that she
has a full complement of passengers, and
that therefore her captain might refuse to
carry the Chinese to Port Townsend. This
placed the citizens in a very awkward po
sition, especially as Treadwell bad sent his
tug-boat to Sitka with a message to the
Governor and to the commander of the
United States cutter Pinta for immediate
relief. It finally occurred to the commit
tee that, as the steamship Idaho was not
due and could not leave Juneau before the
next afternoon, if they could get the Celes
tials as far as Fort Wrangell before the ar
rival of the Idaho there, she would then
take them on to Port Townsend. The more
this question was examined, the more feas
ible it seemed. "For," said the citizens,
"if we keep the coolies here for the Idaho
and her officers should refuse to carry them,
we would then be obliged to bold them for
the steamship Ancon, which steamer will
not arrive before two weeks. In the mean
time, the Governor, with the United States
cutter Pinta will have arrived, when, as a
matter of course, the Chinese will be re
stored to Treadwell. Onr present victory
would then be lost, which would leave ns
in a very unhappy condition."
OwiDg to these circumstances, as there
were two schooners riding at anchor in the
inlet, the committee decided upon sending
the coolies to WraDgell. A contract was
then made with the captain of the Nellie
Martin to carry them for $5 per head, but
Captain Hnhy, owner of the Charlie, re
fused to allow his vessel to go. The com
mittee, however, took possession of her, al
lowing her owner $5 for each coolie put
aboard. The schooners were then brought
to the wharf, and forty coolies pi^ on board
of the Charlie and thirty-six on board of
the Nellie Martin, making in all seventy
six Chinese shipped to Fort Wrangell. The
committee then furnished them with be
tween forty and fifty sacks of rice, some
tea and flour. The schooners were now
ready lor the trip.
Up to this moment, even the elements
seemed to sanction the expulsion of the
Chinese. Now, however, a black cloud
seemed to be gathering form from the
southwest. About 11:30 p. in. (five hours
from the arrival of the Chinese on the
wharf at Juneau) the schooners cast oil'
for Wrangell. At least three hundred of
the citizens lifted their hats, waving them
three cheers, with a hope that they might
bave a quick passage to W raDgell.
I must here say 1 have been through all
the frontier settlements of the United
States, have visited nearly all of our min
ing camps, ami have never met
A MQKE QUIET AND ORDERLY PEOPLE
in a frontier community than t! e citizens
of Junean City and Douglas Island. Dur
ing three consecutive years I bay© only
heard of one street or barroom jgfcl.
About twenty minutes after the vessels
had cast off. a heavy squall broke upon
them from the southwest, which finally
developed into a head wind. This, as the
the citizens knew, would probably prevent
the schooners from getting ont of the inlet
on that tide; still, trusting in the Master of
the elements, they retired that night hop
ing that the wind would change in favor
of the vessels. They were discovered next
morning, however, near the outlet of the
inlet, about six miles from Juneau. About
that 8 a. m., Treadwell boarded both ves
sels and had an interview with the coolies,
but the nature thereof, though well under
stood, did not leak oat. About 10 a. m.
the steamship Idaho arrived at Juneau,
when her captain agreed to take the coolies
to Port Townsend, provided they paid their
fare. Mr. Wheelock and two other mem
bers of the committee then visited the is
land, when they arranged with the coolie
bosses for their fare to Townsend. The
1 steamship Idaho had now dropped anchor
! at Donglas Island, when Treadwell had an
I interview with Captain Hunter; and, to
Î the very great astonishment of the com
mittee, he (Captain Hunter) now refused
! to either tow the vessels to Wrangell or
take the Chinese aboard his steamer. And
about 7 p. m., the 7tb, the Idaho, in leav
ing the inlet, steamed by both vessels with
out even a salutation.
The foregoing is all that can be known
of either the imprisonment of the Chilkat
I chief or the expulsion of the Chinese trom
I Juneau City and Douglas Island until the
return trip of the steamship Ancon, two
I weeks hence, when I shall hope that we
• may hear of the release of the chieftain
: and of the laudiDg of the coolies at Port
Townsend.
JOHN McCAFFERTY.
"BY THE SOUNDING SEA."
Echoes from the Billows that Break
Against the Kocks on the Atlantic
Oo&st.
"Kollin" Describe» the Famous V acht
Knee at Newport and Notes the
Attractive Features of That
Popular Kesort.
[SPECIAL HERALD CORRESPONDENCE.)
Newport, K. I., August 14,1886.
With the "ripple of wave and hiss of
fpray" still sounding in my ears and with
my face still burning from the combined
influence of sun and w ind, I seat myself at
my desk to once more send greeting from
the East to the West. My last letter dealt
with the social side of Newport. This shall
leave the world, the llesh and the devil
alone and flavor of sea, land and other in
animate things. But can I rightfully in
clude yachts under the latter head ? Sure
ly not, for of all graceful, seemingly ani
mate things a yacht heads the list—not a
steam yacht, holding its way in lofty in
difference to wind or tide—but one of those
superbly appointed sloops or schooners that
dash through the blue waters of this bay,
leaving a wake of white foam tossing and
boiling in miniature whirlpools behind
them. Surely they must be endowed with
life and intelligence, so nimbly do they
answer to the touch of the helmsmans
hand. But closely as they are allied to
life, adjuncts as they are to society, they
shall be admitted to the pages of my letter.
How else could I describe the great race
I have just witnessed and which has vir
tually decided the contestant for the in
ternational trophy. It was
A GLORIOUS RACE.
A spanking breeze drove the fleet along
at an astonishing pace and they swept out
of the bay in a magnificent phalanx. Out
side the shelter of the breakwater and
away from the lee of Fort Adams, whose
ramparts were lined with onlookers, the
yachts first felt the full effects of the wind.
How gracefully they bent to it aûd then
surged forward, rising and falling upon the
blue waves, topped with gleaming white
caps. Indeed it is a race for supremacy.
The four great sloops, "Puritan," (winner
of the cup from the "Genesta" last year),
"Priscilla," "Mayflower" and "Atlantic,"
each striving her utmost that she may he
honored by being chosen to contend with
the "Galatea" and hold the championship ;
so nobly won by the "Puritan." Now the 1
laud fades away on either side. The fleet
has become scattered in every direction.
In front opens out the expanse of ocean,
with far down on the horizon a speck : just
visible as it rolls to and fro. This is the
lightship off the "Hen and Chickeus"
shoals, and it is to this as a turning point
the yachts are bound. The wind is still
freshening. Some of the smaller yachts
take in their topsails. One or two drop
out altogether and put.back for the shelter
of the harbor. But the big ones keep all
sail cracked on. The Atlantic leads thus
far, and the hearts of the New Yorkers re
joice within them. But both the May
flower and the Puritan are creeping up
slowly, and just before the stake boat
is rounded the Mayflower forges ahead.
What a picture she makes as she rounds
the lightship—heeled over until the water
pours in a steady stream along her gun
wale. Her crew lying at full length on
the windward side, with sand bags tightly
grasped, ready to shift with the least possi
ble delay as she comes about. Above, an
enormous cloud of canvass, white as snow,
envelops the slender spars. All four are
around the lightship now, and are on the
homeward run. One short reach to wind
ward, and then a long stant almost before
the wind, and the roce is finished with
THE MAY'FLOW ER FIRST,
Puritan second, and Priscilla and Atlantic
straggling along third and fourth. Boston's
cop is fall and New York's enthusiasm is
considerably dampened. However, conso
lation remains in the promise of the future
trial races, and so hope comes to the rescue
of the downhearted ones.
There is no better place in the world for
yachting than the waters that surround
Newport. Do you want smooth water?
You can sail up the hay for twenty miles.
Or do you prefer a rougher trip ? A half
hour's sail will bring you clear of all shel
tering lands into the ocean itself, and then,
of course, your horizon is unkou nded. A
private cruise is around the Island, and
more than once have I stood on Eastman s
lieach, on the ocean side, and watched the
entire squadron of the New ^oik and
Seawanaka yacht clubs creeping by in a
long line. The sunlight glancing on their
snowy sails and bringing them out in bold
relief against the dark blue of the sur
rounding sea. The sea has a great lascina
tion in Newport. Whether it is watched
from the cliffs, where one can stand a hun
dred feet above it and see the billows race
in at headlong speed to dash themselves
into foamy fragments at their base; or
whether one sits on the sandy beaches and
lazily gives ear to the more gentle swish
swash of the waves as they ebb and flow
And to mention the cliffs is to speak of
NEWPORT'S GREATEST PRIDE
LoDg years ago it was made law that
all the inhabitants and visitors to the town
should have free right of way along the
clifl's for all time. After several vain at
temps to change this law the cottagers,
who had built residences along the cliffs,
made a virtue of necessity and submitted.
Then sprang up a rivalry amongst them
as to who could make that part of the
path, which lay in their grounds, the most
attractive. The result has been most
beautiful. Here the path winds through
gay colored flower beds. Here through
velvety lawns. Here a wealthy New
Yorker determined to carry off the palm,
leads it through a lovely arch of moss
covered stones with a stream ot water
trickling down them—and always on the
left hand lies the ocean—the same, yet
ever changing. Eor here it breaks direct
ly upon the foot of the cliff you are on,
and further along a little beach ot
glittering white sand uestles at the loot ot
the rocks, and the waves chant their
paean in a softer key. For three miles
aud more the. path winds through such
scenery and ends abruptly at a huge pile
of rocks, which form the southern end of
the island. Here is the famous "spouting
rock," a huge cavity a hundred feet and
more from the sea, through which alter an
easterly storm the waves are forced to a
height of fifty or sixty feet with a hollow,
roaring sound that ean lie heard for miles
around. Inland, Newport is as beautiful
as by the sea. Luxuriant foliage. Lovely
country roads winding through the woods.
Everywhere the eye is met by an alluring
prospect—one does not wonder that the
Indians named it "the Isle of Peace."
It seems indeed a haven of rest. Unfor
tnnately my stay here is limited. Another
day and I must return to hot Gotham, aud
my Newport trip must become a memory.
But one that shall not soon 1'ade.
ROLLIN'.
Montana Press Association and Some
of its Honorary Members.
Charles S. Fee, General Passenger Agent
of the Northern Pacific Railroad, who is an
honorary member of the Montana Press
Association, and who was in attendance at
their meetings in Bozeman, was the re
cipient of many expressions of thankful
ness by the members individually, and
from the association officially, for his active
presence while with them as the agent of
the railroad company, while seeing to their
comfort in every way as they traveled by
special car from Bozeman to Cinnabar and
return.
And even when the press boarded the
stages with the ladies of the party, Mr.
Fee was not happy until he had secured
for the latter seats on the hurricane roof
of the coaches for the last one of them, so
that they could take in the views along
the Gardiner river and the formations of
the National Park. This gentleman re
mained over Sunday with the Press Asso
ciation at the Park Hotel, and returned
with them yesterday by stages and special
car as far as Bozeman, and reached Hel
ena last evening.
The mention of individual memliere of
the Press Association would be incomplete
without a special notice of others of the
most distinguished of the party
whose names are Mrs. Mattie Yost
and Mr. and Mrs. J. C. Savery,
the former a sprightly Montana
correspondent for the eastern press and the
latter the worty possessors of the controll
ing interest iu the Cable gold mine. The
compliments of Mr. and Mrs. J. C. Savery
were tendered to each member of the As
sociation for a dinner, on Sunday, at the
Mammoth Hot Springs Hotel, aud accepted
most heartily by all the ladies and gen
tlemen of the party, to the number of 28,
who sat down to a table of good things,
where wines and champagnes were the
nectars in which the good health and long
lives of the host and hostess were toasted.
Mrs. Yost and Mr. and Mrs. Savery
are honorary members of the Association
by virtue of their connection with the
press.
The meeting of the Montana Press As
sociation began at Bozeman on the 19th,
and terminating at the National Park on
Monday, the 22d inst., will be remem
bered as one of delightful memories and
happy incidents.
A New Cattle Market.
One of the direct benefits arising from a
visit to St. Paul last winter of the Mon
tana and Dakota cattle men is an exten
sive market at that place, which will be
opened up by an incorporated company
with a capital of a million and a half of
dollars. The company has secured ample
grounds in West St. Paul, where there are
under obstruction immense feed stables,
slanghtering and packing houses and other
appliances for the purchase and feeding of
large numbers of cattle and sheep, for sale
and for packing. It will be good news for
the cattle men to know that they will
have another extensive market 410 miles
nearer home than that of Chicago. The
outgrowth of the vast improvements re
quired for this new industry in St. Paul is
the construction of cable roads that will
require $200,000 of an outlay. These aud
other enterprises show the scope and pos
sibilities of the capitalists who have made
the great city of St. Paul as she appears
before the world to day.
THE many friends of Henry Yillard will
be glad to know that he has so far ^covered
his fortune that he is reckoned to be worth
a quarter million. He has recovered his
fine palatial residence in New York City
and will be back to live in this country in
October. _
Drowned.
Minneapolis, August 21.—A small row
boat was upset on Lake Minnetonka dur
ing the storm this evening and three of the
four men in it were drowned. Those
drowned were Robert T. Hurlhut, a prom
inent real estate dealer, W. B. Jackson, Jr.,
secretary and treasurer of the Monitor
Plow Works Co., and a hired man in the
employ of the plow works.
GRAND ARMY TRAIN.
Arrival and Reception of Commander-in
chief Fairchild and 200 Other Com
rades of the Grand Army.
They Stop lor an Hoor at the Depot
and are Welcomed by the G. A.
1C., S. of V. and Citizens
of Helena.
Intelligence being received that the
Commander-in-Chief of the Grand Army
of the Republic and other distinguished
members of the same honorable order
would pass through Helena on Saturday
morning's train, the members of Wadsworth
Post No 3, G. A. R-, and U. S. Grant Camp
No. 1., S. of V., decided to meet them at
the depot and welcome them to Helena.
The train was
FOUR HOURS LATE
and pulled into the depot precisely at 1
o'clock this afeernoon. It was composed of
fifteen cars, loaded mostly with Grand
Army people, and as it slowed np at the
station the dram corps of theSonsof ^ eter
ans, eleven strong, greeted its arrival with
the spiriting strains of the old marching
tune, "The Girl I Left Behind Me." Wads
worth Post veterans were assembled in foice
and when the train arrived, Post Com
mauder J. G. Sanders, accompanied by
Captain Geo. Gibbs, of the Sous of Vet
erans, sought the commander-in-chief, in
the[Pnllman, and escorted that official anti
several other distinguished members to a
shady) nook on the platform of the depot.
Here they met and chatted with many
ladies and gentlemen of Helena, and
passed a few moments in the exchange ol
friendly greetings. Then a
ROSTRUM WAS IMPROVISED
out of a coopie of tranks placed on a
truck, and upon ihio platform Col. W. F.
Sanders, of Helena, mounted and opened
the informal meeting by extending to the
Commander-in-Chief an invitation to ad
dress the assemblage. General Fairchild
responded graciously, and from the same
rostrum delivered a short speech, thanking
the people of Helena and the local mem
bers of the Grand Army for their cordial
reception. His commanding figure, impas
sioned utterances over the grandness ot
the Union, saved by the noble band of the
Grand Army, the empty sleeve that hung
at his side enclosing the remainder ot an
arm lost in the service of his country—all
touched responsive chords in tl:e breasts ot
the loyal assemblage, and as the veteran
descended from the rostrum a burst of
cheers and applause testified the depth of
the emotions stirred on the occassion.
OTHER SPEEC HES FOLLOWED
in close succession, Col. Handera, with his
accustomed tact and grace, acting as presi
dent of the day and introducing each
speaker with the happiness of expression
and appropos allusions so characteristic of
him. Lack of time and space forbids
special mention of each speech, and
though every one was entirely im
promptu, each was worthyot pre
sentation iu print on account of the
exceptional oratorical ability displayed in
their delivery. The names of the speakers
must suffice. Alter General fairchild the
following distinguished gentlemen made
remarks from the platform in response to
invitation in the order named : Gen. John
C. Black, Colonel of 37th Illinois Infantry
and present Commissioner of Pensions;
General C. II. Grosvenor, member of Con
gress from Ohio ; Ex-Gov. Geo. Hoadley,
of Ohio; Col. E. H. Hicks, of Minneapolis,
Minnesota; Major J. F. Denniston, of
Pittsburg, Pa. Gov. Kobie, of Maine, was
on the train, but when called upon lor a
speech, could not be found.
the closing remarks
were made by Col. Handera. He welcomed
the honored visitors on behalt ot the
Grand Army of Montana and the citizens
of Helena. He bestowed a eulogy upon the
oritorical efforts of the distinguished visi
tors, and closed by tbanking them
in the name of the assemblage
for the kindly spirit manifested in
their gracious responses to the ex
pressed wishes of the citizens for "speeches."
This closed the open air convention, and
the succeeding few minutes before the
train left were employed by the ladies and
gentlemen present in further conversation
with the military tourists.
A Herald reporter gained a short
audience from
GENERAL LUCIUS FAIRCHILD,
the Commander in Chief. The General is
a resident of Madison, Wisconsin. He lost
part of his left arm in the battle of Gettys
burg. He was elected to his pre sentpost
at the recent encampment at San Francisco.
The General spoke in the highest terms of
the receptions the Grand Army had met
throughout the country and expressed his
surprise and pleasure at the greatness of
the Western States and Territories He
also stated that though wines and liquors
flowed in unlimited quantities wherever
they stopped, be had never in the course of
the whole trip seen a Grand Army man in
a state of intoxication.
THE PARTY.
So brief was the stay of the train that it
was impossible to get the names of all the
eminent personages aboard. Among those
not already mentioned was General Helden
Connor, S. Y. Commander-in-Chief, of
Augusta, Maine. The ex-Governor of
Maine still walks with a crutch—the re
minder of what he suffered during the late
war. Other celebrated men from all parts
of the Union were in the party, but their
brief sojourn only allowed personal inter
views with the mast prominent lew.
The number of Grand Army people on
the train was about two hundred, and they
are all going to the National Park.
After that they will go east to their respec
tive homes. They all expressed a desire
that their time were not so limited as to
forbid a stop over at Helena, but tbeir pro
gramme was not changeable, and they had
to proceed.
As the train moved ont at 2 o'clock. Hie
drum corps heat a parting salute, the ladies
and gentlemen of the party waving hats
and handkerchiefs from the car windows
in acknowledgment. It was an occasion
befitting the cordial reception aad demon
stration it evoked.

Academy of Sciences Advocates Pur
chase of Sequoia Lands by the
Government.
A lecture was given last night by
Cloudsley Rutter before the California
Academy of Sciences on "The Natural
History of the Sacramento Salmon." The
speaker dwelt at length on the customs
of the game fish and told of the long dis
tance traversed and the difficulties en
countered by the salmon in reaching- its
spawning place.
The following resolution was adopted by
the society:
Whereas, Many of the citizens of Santa Cruz
Santa tiara ami San Mateo counties and of
the city of San Francisco have urged the es
tablishing of a for»>Et park to cover the "big
liaeln" and the conticuous redwood forests In
the Santa Cruz Mountain?: and whereas, such a
park would secure. If once In the hands of the
United Rt»tes« Government, a large body of
primeval forest of Sequoia seniDervlrens as a
l'crmanent possession for the whole of the peo
ple. This is of special interest to every man
of science, as the time in approaching when
every crimeval redwood • north of San Fran
cisco will be felled by the' lumbermen. And
whereac, truch a purchase will also secure for
the use of the whole people of the peninsula
extending « from Santa. Cruz to San Fianeisco
the use of the mountain waters collected by this
forest arra. <
Resolved, therefore, . That the California
Academy of Sciences' cordially support* the
efforts of the Committee on Ways and Means
recommendlnK an appropriation to secure the
eejrresratlon of the above named territory as a
natural forest park.

During the Spanish-American war he
was a guest at the Hotel de la Poste,
Geneva, Switzerland. Among the other
guests were several German army officers
and one Spanish Government official.
The "Immortalization of Bates" among
Americans In Europe occurred at the
table d'hole dinner one evening. The
Spanish official was seated at Bates' left.
A German officer asked him what he
thought of the war.
"The most brutal nraault," said the
Spaniard, "In the history of the world."
Continuing he said: "The lowest common
street laborer in Spain is so much more of
a gentleman than the average Wall-street

It took tho German officers and half a
grinding yesterday, and United States
Commissioner Heacock declared that he
wished to voice his solemn protest against
the action taken by Collector of the Port
Jackson. The Commissioner insists that
each coolie claiming to h" a "native son"
should be examined Immediately upon his
arrival, so that when he presents himself
in court demanding a landing the courts
may have something upon which to in
volve him in the contradictions which
necessarily will follow from manufactured
testimony. As it is now the yellow "na
tive sons" are sent to the County Jail and
are there coached to tell the lies which

States. And finally. If Collector Jackson
believes this not to be true The Call still
stands ready to prove every charge which
It has made if he will either permit or de
mand an investigation of the Chinese traf
fic under his administration.
Ono of the alleged native-born Chinese
whom Collector Jackson had refused to
examine or pass upon came up for ex
amination yesterday before United States
Court Commissioner Heacock. Because of
the fact that the Chinaman had been
coached in the County Jail and had an
opportunity' to compare his statement
with those of the witnesses provided for
him by his owner he made a good case,
and the Commissioner was obliged to rec
ommend to Judge de Haven that he be
allowed to land as an American citizen.
Heretofore it has been the custom for
a Chinese Inspector from the Chinese
Bureau to board . tfjre incoming steamer
and take down, the statement of the Chi
nese passengers. Whenever any alleged
native-born was refused a landing by the
Collector upon the findings of the bureau
the Immigrant was obliged to petition for
a writ of habeas corpus. The transcript
of the shorthand notes of his statement
was a . club whereby perjury was often
defeated and the' landing denied. But
when Collector Jackson announced that
he would not handle native-born cases
at all he opened a very wide door for the
wholesale admission of coolie laborers
falsely claiming to be American citizens.
United States Commissioner Heacock
said yesterday to a reporter: . "I desire
to enter ray solemn protest against this
condition of affairs. Up to the time that
the examination on shipboard of alleged
native-born was abolished by the Col
lector, the statement made by the im
migrant on board the steamer, before he
had time or opportunity to correspond
with his friends on shore was a great pro
tection to the people of the United States
against the admission of coolie laborers
under the guise of Americans. Now that
protection has been taken away and we
have absolutely nothing on which to base
an effective cross-examination of the Im
migrant or his witnesses. Such a condi
tion; of affairs should not be tolerated.
Every alleged native-born should be ex
amined immediately upon arrival by the
Chinese Bureau in the presence of no one
bat the official stenographer of the bu
reau. Unles3 this is done every alleged
native-born will have an opportuntiy of
landing with impunity. It is only when
they are examined Immediately upon
their arival that the courts have any op
portunity ¦whatever to detect and to frus
trnte conspiracy."
The Secretary of the Treasury ' has di
rected Collector Jaokson to detail an In
spector from the Chinese Bureau to at
tend to the making out of papers for Chi
nese merchants domiciled in this country
who are on the eve of departing for
China with the intention of returning.
They will have these papers on their re
turn to this country to facilitate their*
landing. .
